stem: A 19-year-old male university student is brought to the ED by his parents after eight months of progressive social withdrawal, falling grades, and disorganised thinking. Over the past three weeks he has insisted his housemates are filming him through the smoke alarm and has stopped eating meals they prepare. He has had no prior psychiatric contact, denies suicidal ideation, and has used 1 to 2 cones of cannabis weekly for the past year (last use four weeks ago). He drinks alcohol rarely. There is no family history of psychotic illness. On MSE he is guarded, makes fleeting eye contact, and describes 'the voice of an old teacher' commenting on his actions in the third person. He denies any plan to harm himself or others. Examination is unremarkable. Bloods including FBC, UEC, LFTs, TSH, B12, and urine drug screen are all within normal limits.

data_table.Examination: Alert, oriented, no neurological signs, weight 64 kg (down 5 kg over 6 months)
data_table.Investigations: FBC normal, UEC normal, TSH 1.8 mU/L, B12 412 pmol/L, urine drug screen negative for amphetamines, cocaine, opiates; cannabinoids negative

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in his care pathway?

options.text: Commence olanzapine 5 mg nocte and arrange routine GP follow-up in two weeks
options.rationale: Initiating antipsychotic without specialist assessment and formal early-intervention plan is below AU standard of care for FEP; specialist team needed. Would never be correct (Source: EPPIC; RANZCP).

options.text: Refer to the local early-psychosis (headspace / EPPIC-aligned) service for assertive multidisciplinary assessment
options.rationale: Australian guidance prioritises specialist early-psychosis services with multidisciplinary outreach, low-dose second-generation antipsychotic, psychological therapy and family work to shorten duration of untreated psychosis, which is the strongest modifiable prognostic factor (Source: Orygen EPPIC Model of Care; RANZCP Schizophrenia CPG 2016).

options.text: Diagnose cannabis-induced psychotic disorder and discharge with brief intervention on cessation
options.rationale: Symptoms persist 4 weeks after last cannabis use with negative urine screen, exceeding typical resolution for substance-induced psychosis. Would be correct only with shorter symptom course (Source: DSM-5).

options.text: Arrange outpatient MRI brain and EEG before any psychiatric treatment is offered
options.rationale: Routine neuroimaging and EEG are not required prior to treatment in young adult with typical FEP, normal neurology and normal screen. Would be correct only with focal neuro signs (Source: RANZCP).

options.text: Admit involuntarily under the relevant state Mental Health Act for assessment
options.rationale: Involuntary admission requires risk not manageable less restrictively; engaged with family, denies SI/HI, willing to accept treatment. Would never be correct (Source: MHA).

explanation.summary: Eight months of attenuated symptoms followed by persistent third-person auditory hallucinations and delusions, despite four weeks of abstinence and a negative urine screen, is a typical first-episode psychosis presentation. Australian early-psychosis services deliver assertive, multidisciplinary care that shortens duration of untreated psychosis and improves functional outcomes (Source: Orygen EPPIC Model of Care).
key_points: Duration of untreated psychosis is the strongest modifiable prognostic factor (Orygen EPPIC).
Substance-induced psychosis typically resolves within 1 month of abstinence (RANZCP CPG 2016).
Least-restrictive principle governs decisions about involuntary admission under state MHAs (NSW MHA 2007).
explanation.pearls: Negative urine drug screen plus symptoms persisting beyond 4 weeks of abstinence argues against a primary substance-induced psychotic disorder.

stem: A 23-year-old man with a confirmed diagnosis of schizophrenia after his second psychotic episode is being prescribed maintenance antipsychotic therapy following discharge. He has a BMI of 31 kg/m2, fasting glucose 5.9 mmol/L, HbA1c 41 mmol/mol, triglycerides 2.6 mmol/L and a strong family history of type 2 diabetes (mother and maternal uncle). He smokes 15 cigarettes daily and is sexually active in a stable relationship; adherence in his first episode was poor because of sedation and weight gain on olanzapine 15 mg nocte (he gained 9 kg in three months). He works irregular nightfill shifts and reports that any morning dose 'knocks him out till lunchtime'. On MSE residual paranoid ideation is present but he is no longer responding to internal stimuli. Sexual side effects, akathisia history, and prolactin elevation were specifically asked about and were not problematic. He attends the early-psychosis service weekly He has read about the metabolic risks of olanzapine and clozapine and specifically asks for an antipsychotic with low weight-gain and sedation liability that can be given as a single morning dose so he can sleep through the day after his nightfill shifts. The early-psychosis psychiatrist is available for the prescribing decision today.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 78, BP 132/82, BMI 31 kg/m2, waist 104 cm
data_table.Investigations: Fasting glucose 5.9 mmol/L, HbA1c 41 mmol/mol, total chol 5.4 mmol/L, triglycerides 2.6 mmol/L, HDL 0.9 mmol/L, prolactin 280 mIU/L
data_table.Medications: Olanzapine 15 mg nocte (current), nicotine replacement patch 21 mg/24 h

lead_in: Which maintenance antipsychotic is most appropriate to switch to?

options.text: Aripiprazole 10 mg mane, titrating to 15 mg
options.rationale: Aripiprazole has the most favourable metabolic profile among second-generation antipsychotics, with minimal weight, glucose, lipid or prolactin effects, and is the preferred switch when metabolic burden and adherence are the limiting factors (Source: eTG Psychotropic: Antipsychotic choice; AMH 2024).

options.text: Risperidone 2 mg orally twice daily titrated as needed
options.rationale: Risperidone causes intermediate weight gain and highest prolactin elevation of common atypicals, with sexual dysfunction and gynaecomastia that often drive non-adherence. Would be correct only without metabolic concerns (Source: AMH).

options.text: Quetiapine XR 400 mg nocte titrated over one week
options.rationale: Quetiapine is highly sedating with significant weight and metabolic risk; short half-life and high doses for antipsychotic efficacy. Would be correct only when sedation desired (Source: AMH).

options.text: Amisulpride 400 mg orally each morning titrated as required
options.rationale: Amisulpride is effective and metabolically neutral but causes marked hyperprolactinaemia and is renally excreted; sedation and metabolic burden criteria favour aripiprazole. Would be correct in alternative settings (Source: AMH).

options.text: Clozapine 12.5 mg nocte titrated by registered protocol
options.rationale: Clozapine is reserved for treatment-resistant schizophrenia after failure of at least 2 adequate trials; patient has not had 2 trials. Would be correct only in TRS (Source: RANZCP).

explanation.summary: When metabolic risk, sedation and adherence dominate, aripiprazole is the preferred second-generation antipsychotic in Australian practice because of its negligible weight, glucose, lipid and prolactin effects (Source: eTG Psychotropic).
key_points: Olanzapine and clozapine: highest metabolic burden.
Risperidone and amisulpride: highest prolactin elevation.
Aripiprazole: metabolically neutral, partial D2 agonist.
Clozapine reserved for treatment-resistant schizophrenia after two adequate trials.

stem: A 27-year-old man is brought to ED 18 hours after his first ever dose of intramuscular haloperidol 5 mg, which was given in a different ED for severe agitation related to methamphetamine intoxication. He has since received olanzapine 10 mg orally. He reports that his neck has 'twisted to the right and is stuck' over the past three hours and he cannot move it back; his eyes have rolled upwards intermittently. He is distressed and clutching his jaw. He denies fever, rigidity elsewhere, or confusion. There is no past history of psychiatric illness. Examination shows sustained right-sided torticollis, intermittent oculogyric movements, and clenching of the masseters with normal power in the limbs and no rigidity. He is afebrile, alert and oriented. Urine drug screen earlier was positive for amphetamines only. ECG shows sinus rhythm, QTc 430 ms He has no known glaucoma and no urinary retention.

data_table.Examination: Sustained right torticollis, oculogyric crisis, jaw clenching, no limb rigidity, normal reflexes, GCS 15
data_table.Investigations: ECG QTc 430 ms, sodium 138, potassium 4.1, creatinine 78, CK 220, urine drug screen positive amphetamines

lead_in: What is the single best next intervention?

options.text: Intramuscular benztropine 1 to 2 mg, repeated if no response in 20 minutes
options.rationale: Acute dystonic reaction to high-potency D2 blockade responds rapidly to parenteral anticholinergic; benztropine 1 to 2 mg IM is first-line in Australian practice and onset is within 15 to 20 minutes (Source: eTG Psychotropic: Movement disorders due to antipsychotics; AMH 2024).

options.text: Oral diazepam 10 mg and reassurance, expecting resolution within 24 hours
options.rationale: Benzodiazepines are second-line and slower onset; oral absorption is unreliable with oromandibular dystonia; definitive parenteral anticholinergic preferred. Would be correct only when anticholinergic contraindicated (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: Oral propranolol 20 mg three times daily as monotherapy for the movement disorder
options.rationale: Propranolol treats akathisia not acute dystonia; akathisia is restless inability to sit still vs sustained muscle contractions. Would be correct in akathisia (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: Intravenous dantrolene with active cooling for presumed malignant hyperthermia
options.rationale: Dantrolene with cooling is NMS management (hyperthermia, generalised rigidity, autonomic instability, altered consciousness). Would be correct in NMS (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: Cease all antipsychotics and admit for 48 hours of observation without active treatment
options.rationale: Cessation alone does not abort established dystonia (persists hours to days); targeted anticholinergic is standard. Would never be correct (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).

explanation.summary: Sustained torticollis, oculogyric crisis and jaw clenching within 24 hours of high-potency D2 blockade is acute dystonia. Parenteral benztropine is first-line in Australian practice and resolves symptoms within minutes (Source: eTG Psychotropic).
key_points: Acute dystonia: hours to days after antipsychotic; treat with IM benztropine 1 to 2 mg.
Akathisia: subjective restlessness; treat by reducing dose, then propranolol or benzodiazepine.
Tardive dyskinesia: months to years; consider VMAT2 inhibitor and clozapine switch.
NMS: hyperthermia, rigidity, autonomic instability, altered sensorium.
explanation.pearls: Young men receiving high-potency typical antipsychotics are at highest risk of acute dystonia, including life-threatening laryngeal dystonia.

stem: A 34-year-old woman with bipolar I disorder was commenced on risperidone 2 mg BD seven days ago for an acute manic episode. She returns to the community mental-health team reporting intolerable inner restlessness for the past five days; she cannot sit through a meal or watch television and feels she 'must keep moving or jump out of her skin'. She paces during the interview and shifts weight from foot to foot when standing. There is no involuntary contracture, no tremor at rest, no oculogyric movements and no rigidity. She is afebrile with normal vitals. She continues to take lithium 750 mg nocte (lithium 0.8 mmol/L last week) and is not on any anticholinergic. Suicidal ideation is now emerging, which she relates entirely to the restlessness and not to mood. MSE shows euthymic affect, no psychotic features, and full insight that 'the medication is causing this'.

data_table.Examination: Pacing, foot-tapping, no tremor, no rigidity, no dystonia, normal tone and power
data_table.Medications: Risperidone 2 mg BD (day 7), lithium 750 mg nocte (level 0.8 mmol/L last week)

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Add benztropine 2 mg orally twice daily as anticholinergic monotherapy
options.rationale: Anticholinergics are effective for acute dystonia and drug-induced parkinsonism but only modest benefit in akathisia; may worsen cognition and cause urinary retention. Would be correct in dystonia/parkinsonism (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: Reduce risperidone dose and add propranolol 10 to 20 mg three times daily
options.rationale: Akathisia is best managed by reducing the offending agent where possible and adding a lipid-soluble beta-blocker; propranolol 30 to 80 mg daily in divided doses is the standard Australian first-line pharmacological treatment (Source: eTG Psychotropic; RANZCP Schizophrenia CPG 2016).

options.text: Increase lithium dose to target a serum level above 1.0 mmol/L
options.rationale: Lithium does not treat akathisia; higher level increases tremor, nephrotoxicity and toxicity risk; mania is responding. Would never be correct (Source: AMH).

options.text: Switch immediately from risperidone to clozapine under registered protocol
options.rationale: Clozapine is for TRS after failure of two adequate trials; not appropriate for first antipsychotic side effect. Would be correct only in TRS (Source: RANZCP).

options.text: Reassure and continue current regimen, expecting tolerance within four weeks
options.rationale: Untreated akathisia is strongly associated with suicidal ideation and non-adherence; expectant management is unsafe. Would never be correct (Source: RANZCP).

explanation.summary: Subjective restlessness with observable pacing days after starting risperidone is akathisia. Australian first-line management is to reduce the dose if possible and add propranolol; emerging suicidal ideation linked to akathisia is a recognised hazard requiring prompt action (Source: eTG Psychotropic).
key_points: Distinguish akathisia (subjective restlessness) from dystonia, parkinsonism and tardive dyskinesia.
First-line: dose reduction plus propranolol.
Second-line: benzodiazepine (short course) or mirtazapine.
Akathisia is associated with suicide risk - never dismiss as 'anxiety'.

stem: A 41-year-old man with schizoaffective disorder is admitted to ED from a residential facility. Over the preceding four days haloperidol was uptitrated from 5 mg to 15 mg daily and depot zuclopenthixol acetate 100 mg IM was given two days ago. He has become progressively unwell over 48 hours with high fevers, marked rigidity, profuse sweating, and confusion. Staff report he was holding postures for several minutes earlier that day but is now stiff and barely interactive. He takes no other regular medications and there is no over-the-counter or illicit drug use. On examination he is somnolent and only grunts in response. He has generalised lead-pipe rigidity, no clonus, and reduced deep-tendon reflexes. Pupils are mid-sized and reactive. He is profoundly diaphoretic. ECG shows sinus tachycardia with no acute ischaemia He is on no serotonergic medications, no SSRI, no SNRI, no MAOI, no tramadol and no St John's wort. Bloods show creatine kinase 5400 U/L, urea 12 mmol/L, creatinine 145 micromol/L, and white cell count 13.2. The medical and psychiatric teams are now debating whether this is neuroleptic malignant syndrome or serotonin syndrome, given the rigidity, hyperthermia and altered mental state.

data_table.Examination: GCS 10 (E2 V2 M6), generalised lead-pipe rigidity, no clonus, normal-mid pupils, profuse diaphoresis
data_table.Investigations: WCC 18 x10^9/L, Na 142, K 4.2, Cr 168 micromol/L (baseline 80), CK 8200 U/L, AST 220, lactate 3.1 mmol/L, urine myoglobin positive

lead_in: Which feature most strongly distinguishes neuroleptic malignant syndrome from serotonin syndrome in this presentation?

options.text: Documented core temperature greater than 40 degrees Celsius on presentation
options.rationale: Both NMS and severe serotonin syndrome cause hyperthermia above 40; temperature alone does not discriminate. Would never be the discriminator (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: Recent high-potency D2 antagonist escalation with lead-pipe rigidity and hyporeflexia
options.rationale: NMS classically follows D2 antagonist exposure or dose increase with bradykinesia, generalised lead-pipe rigidity and reduced reflexes; serotonin toxicity produces hyperreflexia, clonus (especially lower limb) and shivering with a serotonergic agent (Source: eTG Psychotropic; Hunter Serotonin Toxicity Criteria).

options.text: A markedly elevated creatine kinase with associated acute kidney injury
options.rationale: Rhabdomyolysis and AKI occur in severe forms of both syndromes; CK is supportive but not discriminating. Would never be the strongest discriminator (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: Altered conscious state with autonomic instability such as labile blood pressure
options.rationale: Both syndromes feature altered conscious state and autonomic instability; necessary but non-specific. Would never be discriminating (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: Sustained posturing earlier in the illness, raising suspicion of catatonia
options.rationale: Posturing can occur in NMS, malignant catatonia, and extreme serotonin toxicity with substantial overlap with catatonia; not the cleanest discriminator. Would never be the strongest (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).

explanation.summary: The discriminating neurological signs are lead-pipe rigidity with hyporeflexia (NMS) versus hyperreflexia and inducible/spontaneous clonus (serotonin toxicity); the medication trigger is a D2 antagonist for NMS and a serotonergic drug for serotonin syndrome (Source: Hunter Serotonin Toxicity Criteria; eTG Psychotropic).
key_points: NMS: D2 antagonist, days, lead-pipe rigidity, hyporeflexia, slower evolution.
Serotonin toxicity: serotonergic agent, hours, clonus, hyperreflexia, shivering.
Malignant catatonia: prior catatonic features, may merge clinically with NMS.
Both: hyperthermia, AKI from rhabdomyolysis, autonomic instability.

stem: A 52-year-old woman is brought to ED two hours after a colleague found her shivering, agitated and unsteady at work. She was started on sertraline 50 mg daily eight weeks ago for major depression. Three days ago her GP added tramadol 50 mg up to four times daily for an acute lumbar strain; she has taken one tablet six-hourly. There is no history of recent illicit drug use. She is alert but restless, sweating, and reports intermittent muscle twitching in her legs. Examination shows mydriasis, intermittent spontaneous clonus in the ankles, brisk patellar reflexes, and resting tremor of the upper limbs. She is afebrile at triage but on rechecking 30 minutes later her temperature has risen to 38.4 degrees. Pupils are 6 mm and reactive. She has no rigidity, no oculogyric movements, and her mental state is anxious but oriented and cooperative

data_table.Examination: Mydriasis 6 mm, spontaneous ankle clonus, hyperreflexia, tremor, shivering, no rigidity, GCS 15
data_table.Investigations: Sodium 138, potassium 4.0, creatinine 84, CK 480 U/L, lactate 1.8 mmol/L, urine drug screen negative
data_table.Medications: Sertraline 50 mg daily (8 weeks), tramadol 50 mg QID (3 days)

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate management?

options.text: Cease serotonergic agents, give IV fluids, oral diazepam 5 mg, observe on monitored bed
options.rationale: Mild to moderate serotonin toxicity is treated by withdrawing serotonergic agents, supportive care including IV fluids and cooling, and benzodiazepines for agitation and clonus; cyproheptadine is reserved for moderate-to-severe cases not responding to supportive care (Source: eTG Psychotropic: Serotonin toxicity; Hunter Serotonin Toxicity Criteria).

options.text: Oral bromocriptine 2.5 mg three times daily with active cooling on the ward
options.rationale: Bromocriptine is dopamine agonist used in some NMS protocols, not serotonin toxicity; this patient's hyperreflexia, clonus and serotonergic medication history indicate serotonin syndrome. Would be correct in NMS (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: Intramuscular benztropine 2 mg as treatment for the movement disorder
options.rationale: Benztropine treats acute dystonia and parkinsonism from D2 blockade; no role in serotonin toxicity and anticholinergic effects can worsen hyperthermia. Would be correct in dystonia (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: Intravenous dantrolene with intubation and neuromuscular paralysis
options.rationale: Dantrolene and neuromuscular blockade are reserved for life-threatening hyperthermia over 41.5 not responding to supportive measures; aggressive escalation here is premature. Would be correct only at life-threatening hyperthermia (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: Switch sertraline to venlafaxine and reduce the tramadol dose by half today
options.rationale: Both venlafaxine and tramadol are serotonergic; switching to another SNRI while continuing tramadol perpetuates toxicity. Would never be correct (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).

explanation.summary: Spontaneous lower-limb clonus plus a serotonergic agent meets Hunter Criteria for serotonin toxicity. Management is cessation of all serotonergic agents, supportive care, and benzodiazepines, with cyproheptadine for non-responders (Source: Hunter Serotonin Toxicity Criteria; eTG Psychotropic).
key_points: Hunter Criteria require a serotonergic agent plus one of: spontaneous clonus, inducible clonus with agitation/diaphoresis, ocular clonus, tremor with hyperreflexia, or hypertonia with T above 38 and ocular/inducible clonus.
Tramadol, fentanyl, MAOIs, linezolid, St John's wort all contribute to serotonin load.
Benzodiazepines are first-line pharmacotherapy.
Cyproheptadine 12 mg loading then 2 mg q2h for moderate toxicity not responding.

stem: A 29-year-old man with schizophrenia has had persistent third-person auditory hallucinations and persecutory delusions for the past 18 months despite engagement with the community team. He has completed an adequate trial of olanzapine 20 mg daily for 8 weeks (adherence confirmed by plasma level) with only minor reduction in symptoms and intolerable weight gain, followed by aripiprazole 30 mg daily for 7 weeks with adherence confirmed but minimal response. He has been engaged in CBT for psychosis fortnightly. He has no medical comorbidities, an ECG shows normal sinus rhythm with QTc 410 ms, FBC is normal and he has no history of cardiac, haematological or seizure disorders. He lives with his parents who are willing to support a medication change, and he is currently in regular outpatient contact with his case manager He is willing to commit to weekly FBC monitoring and to admission for induction if needed. He has no history of cardiomyopathy and an echocardiogram shows preserved ejection fraction He understands clozapine monitoring and is willing to commence today Augmentation strategies were considered and not chosen His parents support the change.

data_table.Examination: BMI 28 kg/m2, no neurological signs, no oedema
data_table.Investigations: FBC normal (WCC 6.4, neutrophils 3.8 x10^9/L), UEC normal, ECG sinus rhythm QTc 410 ms, troponin not measured (asymptomatic)

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step in pharmacotherapy?

options.text: Switch to clozapine, initiated under registered protocol with weekly FBC monitoring
options.rationale: Treatment-resistant schizophrenia is defined as failure of two adequate trials (>=6 weeks at therapeutic dose, adherence confirmed) of two different antipsychotics; clozapine is the only evidence-based pharmacotherapy and Australian use requires patient registration with a clozapine monitoring service (Source: RANZCP Schizophrenia CPG 2016; AMH 2024).

options.text: Add a second oral antipsychotic, amisulpride 400 mg, to the current aripiprazole regimen
options.rationale: Antipsychotic polypharmacy is not first-line for TRS and increases adverse effects without consistent efficacy benefit. Would never be correct (Source: RANZCP).

options.text: Trial a third oral antipsychotic, risperidone 6 mg daily, for 8 weeks first
options.rationale: Deferring clozapine after two adequate trials prolongs symptom burden, functional impairment and suicide risk. Would never be correct (Source: RANZCP).

options.text: Refer for electroconvulsive therapy as primary management of the persistent psychotic symptoms
options.rationale: ECT is for catatonia, severe depression with psychosis, or augmentation in clozapine-resistant; not first-line for TRS. Would be correct after clozapine failure (Source: RANZCP).

options.text: Switch to a long-acting injectable formulation of aripiprazole given monthly intramuscularly
options.rationale: LAIs address adherence not efficacy; adherence has been confirmed, so issue is treatment resistance. Would be correct only with non-adherence (Source: RANZCP).

explanation.summary: Two adequate trials at therapeutic dose with confirmed adherence define treatment-resistant schizophrenia, for which clozapine is the only superior agent. In Australia, clozapine is dispensed via registered programs (e.g. ClopineCentral, ClozaConnect) with mandatory haematological monitoring (Source: RANZCP Schizophrenia CPG 2016).
key_points: TRS criteria: failure of 2+ different antipsychotics at adequate dose and duration with adherence.
Clozapine requires baseline FBC, ECG and registration on a monitoring service.
Polypharmacy and switching to another non-clozapine agent are not first-line.
Clozapine reduces all-cause mortality and suicide in schizophrenia.

stem: A 32-year-old woman has been on clozapine for 16 days, currently titrated to 200 mg nocte. She presents to the early-psychosis clinic feeling unwell for 36 hours with chest tightness on exertion, palpitations, a temperature of 38.2 degrees and a non-productive cough. There is no rash, no pleuritic pain, and no recent viral contact at home. She has no history of cardiac disease, no chest pain at rest, and observations show tachycardia. Examination reveals a faint S3 gallop, no murmurs, clear chest sounds, and no peripheral oedema. Bloods today show WCC 7.8, neutrophils 4.6 x10^9/L (within normal), CRP 78, troponin I 380 ng/L (laboratory upper reference 26 ng/L), eosinophils 0.9 x10^9/L. ECG shows sinus tachycardia at 118, no ST elevation, no PR depression.

data_table.Examination: Faint S3 gallop, clear lung fields, no oedema, no rash
data_table.Investigations: WCC 7.8, neutrophils 4.6, eosinophils 0.9 x10^9/L, CRP 78, troponin I 380 ng/L (URL 26), ECG sinus tachycardia 118, no ST changes

lead_in: What is the single best next intervention?

options.text: Continue clozapine, add aspirin and review in 48 hours with repeat troponin
options.rationale: Continuing clozapine in suspected myocarditis is contraindicated (progression to fulminant HF); aspirin alone is inadequate. Would never be correct (Source: RANZCP).

options.text: Cease clozapine immediately, admit for cardiology review, and arrange urgent echocardiogram
options.rationale: In the first 4 to 8 weeks of clozapine titration, fever with tachycardia and rising troponin and CRP suggests clozapine-induced myocarditis; immediate cessation, admission, troponin trend, ECG, and echocardiogram are mandated (Source: AMH 2024; eTG Psychotropic: Clozapine adverse effects; TGA Clozapine PI).

options.text: Stop clozapine because the neutrophil count of 4.6 x10^9/L indicates impending agranulocytosis
options.rationale: ANC 4.6 is normal; agranulocytosis is under 0.5; clozapine is interrupted (Amber) at 1.0-1.5 and ceased at under 1.0. Would never be correct (Source: ClopineCentral).

options.text: Reduce clozapine to 100 mg and add atenolol 25 mg daily
options.rationale: Dose reduction without cessation does not address myocarditis; beta blockade can mask haemodynamic deterioration. Would never be correct (Source: RANZCP).

options.text: Treat empirically for community-acquired pneumonia with oral amoxicillin and doxycycline
options.rationale: Gallop, raised troponin and normal lung exam point to myocarditis not pneumonia; treating CAP without addressing cardiac picture is wrong. Would be correct only with documented CAP (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).

explanation.summary: Clozapine-induced myocarditis typically occurs in the first 4 to 8 weeks of titration. The Australian recommendation is weekly FBC, troponin and CRP monitoring during this window and immediate cessation if myocarditis is suspected (Source: AMH 2024; Clozapine TGA PI).
key_points: Myocarditis window: weeks 2 to 4 (most), up to 8 weeks.
Red flags: fever, tachycardia, troponin rise, CRP >50, dyspnoea, eosinophilia.
Agranulocytosis cut-offs (general): Green N >=2.0, Amber 1.5 to 2.0, Red <1.5 (some programs use 1.0).
Other clozapine adverse effects: hypersalivation, constipation/ileus, seizures, weight gain, hyperglycaemia.

stem: A 38-year-old man with treatment-resistant schizophrenia has been stable on clozapine 400 mg nocte for 4 years. His mother brings him to ED because he has not opened his bowels for nine days and has progressive abdominal distension, two episodes of vomiting today, and reduced oral intake. He has been on regular docusate and senna at home but his case manager added loperamide 1 week ago after he reported watery stools (in retrospect, likely overflow). On examination he is mildly tachycardic, afebrile, and abdomen is distended, tympanic, with sluggish bowel sounds and diffuse tenderness without peritonism. He has no haematochezia. There is no past history of inflammatory bowel disease or abdominal surgery. He smokes 20 cigarettes daily. His clozapine FBC was normal three days ago His abdominal x-ray shows grossly dilated loops of large bowel with faecal loading and no free gas. The surgical registrar is reviewing him in ED.

data_table.Examination: Distended tympanic abdomen, sluggish bowel sounds, diffuse tenderness, no peritonism, no scars
data_table.Investigations: WCC 11.2, Hb 138, Na 138, K 3.6, creatinine 92, lactate 1.4 mmol/L; AXR: dilated colon with no rectal gas, no free gas
data_table.Medications: Clozapine 400 mg nocte, docusate 50 mg BD, senna 15 mg nocte, loperamide 4 mg/day (1 week)

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next step?

options.text: Cease loperamide, admit for IV fluids, NG decompression, surgical review and escalated laxatives
options.rationale: Clozapine-induced gastrointestinal hypomotility can progress from chronic constipation to ileus, megacolon and bowel perforation; loperamide is contraindicated, and management requires admission, decompression, surgical review and stepwise laxative escalation (Source: eTG Psychotropic: Clozapine adverse effects; AMH 2024).

options.text: Add an oral fibre supplement and arrange general practitioner follow-up in one week
options.rationale: Outpatient management with bulk laxative in established ileus risks bowel perforation; bulking agents can worsen impaction. Would never be correct (Source: RANZCP; AMH).

options.text: Cease clozapine permanently because of constipation risk and switch to another antipsychotic
options.rationale: Abrupt permanent cessation of clozapine in stable treatment-responsive patient risks severe relapse; standard is to manage GI complication. Would never be correct (Source: RANZCP).

options.text: Order an urgent colonoscopy as the primary diagnostic investigation today
options.rationale: Colonoscopy in suspected ileus or megacolon carries high perforation risk; AXR and CT abdomen preferred. Would never be correct as first investigation (Source: ANZ Gastro).

options.text: Prescribe oral metoclopramide as a single 10 mg dose and discharge home
options.rationale: Metoclopramide is D2 antagonist that can cause EPSE and unlikely to reverse established large-bowel hypomotility; discharge unsafe. Would never be correct (Source: AMH).

explanation.summary: Clozapine-induced gastrointestinal hypomotility is under-recognised, can be fatal, and demands aggressive laxative regimens, avoidance of antimotility agents, and prompt admission with imaging when ileus or megacolon is suspected (Source: eTG Psychotropic).
key_points: All clozapine patients need a documented bowel-monitoring plan and prophylactic laxatives.
Avoid loperamide, anticholinergics with high antimuscarinic load, and opioids where possible.
Overflow diarrhoea can mask severe constipation.
Mortality from ileus/perforation in clozapine cohorts exceeds that from agranulocytosis.

stem: A 56-year-old man is admitted to the medical ward after presenting with a fractured wrist sustained in a fall. He drinks approximately 25 standard drinks per day and his last drink was 36 hours ago. He has a history of two previous withdrawal seizures and one prior episode of delirium tremens. He is currently tremulous, diaphoretic and reports tactile hallucinations of insects on his skin. He is oriented to person and place but disoriented to time. His CIWA-Ar score is 22. He has no head injury. He was given 100 mg IM thiamine in ED. He has no known liver decompensation; LFTs show AST 110, ALT 65, INR 1.2 and albumin 36. He has no respiratory disease.

data_table.Examination: Coarse tremor, diaphoresis, tactile hallucinations, oriented to person/place not time, no focal neurology
data_table.Investigations: Glucose 5.6 mmol/L, Na 138, K 3.2, Mg 0.62 mmol/L, AST 110, ALT 65, INR 1.2, alcohol level 0
data_table.Medications: Thiamine 100 mg IM in ED, no regular meds

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in managing his withdrawal?

options.text: Continue daily thiamine 100 mg orally and observe with hourly CIWA-Ar reassessment overnight
options.rationale: CIWA-Ar 22 is severe withdrawal; observation alone risks progression to seizures or DTs; oral thiamine is insufficient prophylaxis. Would never be correct (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).
source_refs: NSW Health Drug and Alcohol Withdrawal Clinical Practice Guidelines

options.text: Symptom-triggered diazepam 10-20 mg PO 1-2 hourly titrated to CIWA-Ar plus IV Pabrinex
options.rationale: Symptom-triggered benzodiazepine dosing is the standard of care for severe alcohol withdrawal in Australian guidance; parenteral high-dose B-complex (Pabrinex) is required for Wernicke's prophylaxis in any heavy drinker with malnutrition risk, and electrolyte correction reduces seizure risk (Source: NSW Health Withdrawal Guidelines; eTG Addiction; AMH 2024).
source_refs: NSW Health Drug and Alcohol Withdrawal Clinical Practice Guidelines

options.text: Commence oral carbamazepine 200 mg three times daily as the sole withdrawal agent
options.rationale: Anticonvulsants other than benzodiazepines are second-line and inferior for severe withdrawal; benzodiazepines are first-line. Would be correct only as adjunct (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).
source_refs: NSW Health Drug and Alcohol Withdrawal Clinical Practice Guidelines

options.text: Use an intravenous propofol infusion in a ward-based setting with bedside monitoring
options.rationale: Propofol infusion is for refractory DT in ICU; not appropriate on ward and not first-line. Would be correct in refractory DT in ICU (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: Restart small amounts of oral alcohol to taper consumption over 5 days at home
options.rationale: Alcohol tapering is not used in AU inpatient practice; pharmacokinetic variability, hepatotoxicity and impaired titration make benzodiazepine-based withdrawal safer. Would never be correct (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).
source_refs: NSW Health Drug and Alcohol Withdrawal Clinical Practice Guidelines

explanation.summary: Severe alcohol withdrawal (CIWA-Ar >15) requires symptom-triggered diazepam, parenteral B-complex for Wernicke's prophylaxis, and aggressive electrolyte correction. Prior seizures or DTs raise relapse risk and justify earlier escalation (Source: NSW Health Withdrawal Guidelines; eTG Addiction).
key_points: Pabrinex (high-dose IV thiamine + B-complex) BD for 3 to 5 days for at-risk patients.
Oral thiamine alone is inadequate in alcoholic malnutrition.
Symptom-triggered diazepam outperforms fixed-dose regimens.
Correct hypomagnesaemia and hypokalaemia to reduce seizure risk.

sources.label: NSW Health Drug and Alcohol Withdrawal Clinical Practice Guidelines

stem: A 62-year-old homeless man is brought to ED after being found drowsy in a park. He has a 30-year history of heavy daily alcohol use and weighs 54 kg with a BMI of 18. He is hypothermic at 35.4 degrees and capillary glucose is 2.8 mmol/L. The triage nurse hangs an IV bag of 5% dextrose. Forty minutes later the resident notes ataxia, horizontal nystagmus on lateral gaze, and ophthalmoparesis with bilateral abducens weakness. He is intermittently confused but rousable. He has had no head trauma. CT brain shows no acute lesion. He has not received any thiamine yet. Sodium is 134, potassium 3.4, magnesium 0.58 mmol/L, INR 1.3, AST 95, ALT 50 The triage nurse asks whether the dextrose infusion should be paused before any thiamine is given, and whether IM 100 mg thiamine is sufficient He has no head trauma history.

data_table.Vital signs: T 35.4, HR 98, BP 108/62, RR 16, SpO2 98%, BSL 2.8 mmol/L
data_table.Examination: Ataxic, horizontal nystagmus, bilateral CN VI weakness, confused, GCS 13 (E3 V4 M6)
data_table.Investigations: Na 134, K 3.4, Mg 0.58 mmol/L, INR 1.3, AST 95, ALT 50, CT brain unremarkable

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: IV high-dose B-complex (Pabrinex 2 pairs IV TDS), correct magnesium, then continue dextrose
options.rationale: Suspected Wernicke's requires immediate parenteral high-dose B-complex (Pabrinex) before or with any glucose; magnesium is a cofactor and must be corrected for thiamine to be effective. Australian dosing is 2 pairs IV TDS for 3 to 5 days (Source: eTG Psychotropic: Wernicke's encephalopathy; NSW Health Withdrawal Guidelines).
source_refs: NSW Health Drug and Alcohol Withdrawal Clinical Practice Guidelines

options.text: Continue dextrose, give a single 100 mg intramuscular dose of thiamine, then monitor
options.rationale: Standard 100 mg IM thiamine is inadequate for established Wernicke's; high-dose parenteral B-complex BD-TDS for several days required. Would be correct only for prophylaxis not established WE (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: Hold any further IV glucose and commence intravenous magnesium sulfate as monotherapy
options.rationale: Correcting hypoglycaemia must continue with concurrent thiamine; magnesium without thiamine does not reverse Wernicke's. Would be correct as part of full regimen, not as monotherapy (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: Arrange an urgent MRI brain before commencing any pharmacological replacement therapy
options.rationale: Wernicke's is clinical diagnosis; delay for imaging risks permanent Korsakoff amnesia; treatment must be empirical. Would never be correct (Source: BMJ Best Practice).

options.text: Commence oral thiamine 300 mg daily without any parenteral B-vitamin therapy
options.rationale: Oral absorption is unreliable in chronic alcohol use and inadequate for established encephalopathy; parenteral high-dose B-complex required. Would never be correct (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).

explanation.summary: Wernicke's encephalopathy classically presents with ophthalmoplegia (often CN VI), ataxia and confusion in a thiamine-deficient patient. Glucose without preceding or concurrent thiamine can precipitate Wernicke's (Source: eTG Psychotropic).
key_points: Empirical high-dose parenteral B-complex (Pabrinex) for any at-risk patient before glucose.
Magnesium is a thiamine cofactor and must be replaced.
Untreated Wernicke's progresses to irreversible Korsakoff syndrome.
Caine criteria: 2 of 4 - dietary deficiency, oculomotor abnormalities, cerebellar dysfunction, altered mental state.

sources.label: NSW Health Drug and Alcohol Withdrawal Clinical Practice Guidelines

stem: A 49-year-old teacher with alcohol use disorder presents to her GP seeking pharmacological help to stay abstinent. She completed a community-supported withdrawal three weeks ago and has been alcohol-free since then. Her main concerns are recurrent strong cravings and a high-risk family wedding in two weeks. She has chronic back pain managed with paracetamol only and no current opioid prescription. She has no significant liver impairment (LFTs and INR normal), no renal impairment, and a normal ECG. She does not have a history of severe liver disease or active hepatitis. She is highly motivated, lives with a supportive partner, and attends weekly counselling. She has tried mutual-support groups previously without sustained benefit. She is otherwise well and not pregnant; she uses an IUD for contraception She has read about acamprosate, naltrexone and disulfiram and asks the GP to recommend the option most likely to help her control cravings, particularly given the high-risk family wedding in two weeks where alcohol will be present She has no opioid prescription and no current opioid use, and she does not want disulfiram because of the wedding.

data_table.Examination: BMI 24, no stigmata of chronic liver disease, normal mental state, no other comorbidities
data_table.Investigations: AST 28, ALT 24, GGT 38, bilirubin 12, INR 1.0, eGFR >90 mL/min, FBC normal
data_table.Medications: Paracetamol 1 g QID PRN, no opioids

lead_in: Which pharmacotherapy is most appropriate as first-line maintenance treatment to support abstinence?

options.text: Naltrexone 50 mg orally daily
options.rationale: Naltrexone is first-line for AUD maintenance in patients not on opioids, reduces cravings and heavy-drinking days, and is appropriate where craving is the dominant symptom; it is contraindicated in current opioid use (Source: RACP/RANZCP Australian Guidelines for the Treatment of AUD; eTG Addiction; AMH 2024).

options.text: Acamprosate 666 mg three times daily
options.rationale: Acamprosate is equally valid first-line (best for sustaining abstinence already achieved); naltrexone has stronger craving evidence. Would be correct when abstinence already achieved (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: Disulfiram 200 mg daily
options.rationale: Disulfiram requires high motivation, supervised dosing and acceptance of aversive reaction; second-line. Would be correct after first-line failure (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: Diazepam 5 mg three times daily for ongoing craving
options.rationale: Benzodiazepines are for acute withdrawal only; ongoing use risks cross-dependence and is not maintenance therapy. Would never be correct (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: Topiramate 100 mg twice daily
options.rationale: Topiramate has some evidence but is off-label in AU and third-line; cognitive adverse effects notable in working woman. Would be correct only after first-line failure (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).

explanation.summary: Naltrexone and acamprosate are first-line PBS-listed pharmacotherapies for AUD in Australia. Naltrexone preferentially reduces cravings and heavy drinking; acamprosate supports continued abstinence. Disulfiram and topiramate are second/third-line (Source: RACGP/RANZCP Australian AUD Guidelines).
key_points: Confirm patient is opioid-free (urine drug screen) before naltrexone.
Acamprosate: maintain abstinence; dosed TDS by weight; renal excretion.
Disulfiram: requires supervised dosing and motivation; CVS/hepatic risks.
Pharmacotherapy is always combined with psychosocial support.

stem: A 58-year-old man has chronic alcohol use disorder and has failed adequate trials of naltrexone (compliance confirmed by his partner) and acamprosate over the past 12 months, with ongoing harmful drinking. He has well-compensated alcohol-related liver disease (Child-Pugh A; AST 80, ALT 55, INR 1.2, albumin 35, no ascites or encephalopathy). He has stable ischaemic heart disease with prior PCI two years ago and a normal ECG today. He is highly motivated, lives with a supportive partner who can supervise daily dosing, and attends a specialist alcohol service. Echocardiogram shows preserved ejection fraction 58%. He has no respiratory disease. He understands and accepts the disulfiram-ethanol reaction The specialist alcohol clinician is keen to start a relapse-prevention pharmacotherapy today; the patient has consented to the disulfiram-ethanol reaction warning leaflet. The cardiology team has flagged that hypotension and arrhythmia risk are the main concern with disulfiram.

data_table.Examination: Stable, no signs of decompensated liver disease, normal mental state
data_table.Investigations: AST 80, ALT 55, INR 1.2, albumin 35, no ascites; ECG normal; EF 58%
data_table.Medications: Aspirin 100 mg daily, atorvastatin 40 mg, metoprolol 25 mg BD; naltrexone and acamprosate trialled previously

lead_in: Which factor most strongly contraindicates initiating disulfiram in this patient?

options.text: Pre-existing ischaemic heart disease with prior coronary intervention
options.rationale: Significant cardiovascular disease (including ischaemic heart disease, severe hypertension, recent MI or stroke) is a contraindication to disulfiram because the disulfiram-ethanol reaction can precipitate severe hypertension, arrhythmia and cardiovascular collapse (Source: eTG Addiction; AMH 2024).

options.text: Mild compensated liver disease with Child-Pugh A staging
options.rationale: Severe hepatic impairment is contraindication; compensated Child-Pugh A is caution requiring LFT monitoring, not absolute bar. Would be correct only with severe impairment (Source: AMH).

options.text: Concurrent metoprolol therapy used for rate control
options.rationale: Metoprolol has no specific contraindication with disulfiram. Would never be correct (Source: AMH).

options.text: Patient age over 55 years at initiation of disulfiram
options.rationale: Age alone is not contraindication; risk-benefit individualised. Would never be correct (Source: AMH).

options.text: Concurrent atorvastatin therapy used for secondary prevention
options.rationale: No clinically significant interaction precluding co-prescription of atorvastatin with disulfiram. Would never be correct (Source: AMH).

explanation.summary: Disulfiram contraindications include significant cardiovascular disease, severe hepatic failure, pregnancy, psychosis and inability to consent to supervised aversion therapy. Australian practice requires informed consent and ideally supervised dosing (Source: eTG Addiction; AMH 2024).
key_points: Avoid in IHD, severe HTN, recent MI/stroke, severe liver failure, psychosis.
Counsel about all ethanol sources (cough mixtures, mouthwash, aftershave, sauces).
LFTs at baseline, 2 and 6 weeks, then 3 to 6 monthly.
Reaction onset is 10 to 30 minutes after ethanol, can last several hours.

stem: A 31-year-old woman with a 6-year history of injecting heroin presents to a community OST clinic seeking opioid agonist treatment. She last used heroin 12 hours ago. She has tested positive for HCV antibody (RNA pending) and her LFTs show ALT 84, AST 60. She has no other psychiatric medications and no significant medical comorbidity. Her observations are normal. COWS score on assessment is 14 (moderate withdrawal) with pupil dilation, sweating, yawning, mild rhinorrhoea, anxiety and bone aches. Urine drug screen is positive for opioids only. She is unable to attend a clinic for daily supervised dispensing because she works variable shifts but can attend twice weekly. She has no respiratory disease, no QT-prolonging medications, and an ECG today shows QTc 410 ms.

data_table.Examination: Mydriasis, diaphoresis, restless, no signs of overdose; venepuncture marks both antecubital fossae
data_table.Investigations: COWS 14, AST 60, ALT 84, ECG QTc 410 ms, urine drug screen positive opioids only, HCV antibody positive (RNA pending)

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in induction?

options.text: Sublingual buprenorphine-naloxone 4 mg now, top-up 2 to 4 mg in 2 hours if withdrawal persists
options.rationale: Buprenorphine is preferred over methadone for community induction because of lower overdose risk, capacity for unsupervised takeaway doses earlier, and shorter induction; it must only be given once moderate withdrawal is established (COWS >=12) to avoid precipitated withdrawal (Source: National Guidelines for Medication-Assisted Treatment of Opioid Dependence; eTG Addiction).
source_refs: National Guidelines for Medication-Assisted Treatment of Opioid Dependence (NGMA)

options.text: Commence methadone 60 mg orally today as the safer first-day community induction dose
options.rationale: Methadone induction starts low (20-30 mg, no more than 40 mg day 1) due to long half-life and accumulation risk; 60 mg day 1 has caused fatal overdoses. Would never be correct (Source: NSW Health Methadone Guideline).
source_refs: National Guidelines for Medication-Assisted Treatment of Opioid Dependence (NGMA)

options.text: Wait until she has been opioid-free for 24 hours and then commence oral naltrexone
options.rationale: Oral naltrexone has very high relapse rates in opioid dependence and is not first-line maintenance; cannot start until 7-10 days opioid-free. Would be correct in highly motivated abstinence-focused patients (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).
source_refs: National Guidelines for Medication-Assisted Treatment of Opioid Dependence (NGMA)

options.text: Give intramuscular naloxone now to confirm opioid dependence before any induction begins
options.rationale: Naloxone reverses overdose, not for confirming dependence; deliberate precipitation of withdrawal is unethical. Would never be correct (Source: NSW Health).
source_refs: National Guidelines for Medication-Assisted Treatment of Opioid Dependence (NGMA)

options.text: Commence sublingual buprenorphine 16 mg as a single first dose to suppress withdrawal rapidly
options.rationale: Day-1 buprenorphine should start at 2-8 mg with cautious top-up; 16 mg risks oversedation. Would be correct only at later titration (Source: NSW Health).
source_refs: National Guidelines for Medication-Assisted Treatment of Opioid Dependence (NGMA)

explanation.summary: Buprenorphine-naloxone induction in established moderate withdrawal (COWS >=12) is preferred for community OST, especially for patients with limited capacity for daily supervised dispensing. Methadone is started low and titrated slowly because of accumulation and overdose risk (Source: NGMA; eTG Addiction).
key_points: Methadone day 1: 20 to 30 mg, max 40 mg; steady state at 5 days.
Buprenorphine: lower overdose risk, ceiling on respiratory depression, faster stabilisation.
Precipitated withdrawal: occurs if buprenorphine given too early.
ECG before methadone if dose >100 mg or QT-prolonging meds; review at 1 month.

sources.label: National Guidelines for Medication-Assisted Treatment of Opioid Dependence (NGMA)

stem: A 45-year-old man on methadone 80 mg daily for 3 years presents to his GP with new-onset palpitations. He was admitted for community-acquired pneumonia 10 days ago and discharged on azithromycin 500 mg daily (now stopped) and oral fluconazole 200 mg daily (continuing) for oropharyngeal candidiasis. He has also been taking diphenhydramine for sleep. Resting ECG today shows sinus rhythm at 78 with QTc 510 ms (previously 430 ms before admission). He has no chest pain, no syncope and no electrolyte derangement. Electrolytes are normal including potassium 4.1 and magnesium 0.85. He has not increased his methadone dose. He denies non-prescribed drug use and urine drug screen is positive only for methadone and benzodiazepines (he disclosed temazepam supplied by a friend)

data_table.Examination: No oedema, no murmurs, clear chest, no syncope
data_table.Investigations: ECG QTc 510 ms (was 430), K 4.1, Mg 0.85, eGFR >90, LFTs normal
data_table.Medications: Methadone 80 mg daily, fluconazole 200 mg daily, diphenhydramine PRN, temazepam (illicitly sourced)

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate management?

options.text: Stop fluconazole and diphenhydramine, counsel against benzodiazepines, consider methadone dose reduction or switch
options.rationale: Fluconazole inhibits methadone metabolism (CYP3A4/CYP2B6) and prolongs QT; diphenhydramine adds anticholinergic load and QT effect; the priority is removing the offending agents, addressing benzodiazepine use, and re-evaluating with repeat ECG, with a switch to buprenorphine if QT remains prolonged (Source: NGMA; eTG Addiction; AMH 2024).
source_refs: National Guidelines for Medication-Assisted Treatment of Opioid Dependence (NGMA)

options.text: Continue the current regimen unchanged and review the patient in 4 weeks with a repeat ECG
options.rationale: QTc 510 ms carries significant torsades risk and demands prompt action; passive review is unsafe. Would never be correct (Source: AMH).

options.text: Increase methadone to 100 mg daily to improve compliance and stabilise opioid receptors
options.rationale: Increasing methadone worsens QT prolongation and overdose risk, particularly with co-ingested benzodiazepines. Would never be correct (Source: AMH).
source_refs: National Guidelines for Medication-Assisted Treatment of Opioid Dependence (NGMA)

options.text: Cease methadone abruptly today and commence naltrexone after 24 hours of opioid abstinence
options.rationale: Abrupt methadone cessation precipitates withdrawal and relapse; naltrexone cannot start until 7-10 days opioid-free. Would never be correct (Source: NSW Health).
source_refs: National Guidelines for Medication-Assisted Treatment of Opioid Dependence (NGMA)

options.text: Add an oral beta-blocker (atenolol 25 mg daily) to reduce torsades de pointes risk acutely
options.rationale: Beta-blockers reduce risk in congenital LQTS but not standard response to drug-induced QT prolongation; correct approach is to identify and remove QT-prolonging drugs. Would be correct only in congenital LQTS (Source: AMH).

explanation.summary: Methadone prolongs QTc and accumulates when CYP3A4 inhibitors (azoles, macrolides, HIV PIs) are introduced; combined with benzodiazepines, overdose risk rises. Australian OST guidance recommends ECG before methadone if dose >100 mg or QT-prolonging meds, repeat at 1 month and after dose or interacting-drug changes (Source: NGMA).
key_points: QT-prolonging interactions: azoles, macrolides, ondansetron, SSRIs, antipsychotics, HIV PIs.
QTc >500 ms is a strong indication to switch from methadone to buprenorphine.
Concurrent benzodiazepines markedly increase overdose mortality.
Naloxone supply and overdose education are part of routine OST care.

sources.label: National Guidelines for Medication-Assisted Treatment of Opioid Dependence (NGMA)

stem: A 26-year-old man is brought to ED by police after acting bizarrely in a shopping centre, smashing a window and shouting at unseen individuals. He has used crystal methamphetamine for 18 months and admits to using last night. On arrival he is pacing, sweating, threatening staff verbally, and intermittently striking the wall. He has dilated pupils, marked tachycardia, hypertension and a low-grade fever. He has not had any seizures, no head injury, and there is no other identified medical contributor. He is on no regular medication. Initial verbal de-escalation has failed and he is now attempting to leave. Capillary glucose is 6.8 mmol/L. Bloods are pending. The patient weighs approximately 80 kg, has no airway concerns at present, and continuous cardiac monitoring can be commenced in the high-acuity area

data_table.Examination: Mydriasis, diaphoresis, agitated, hyper-vigilant, no focal neurology, no head injury, no obvious overdose features
data_table.Investigations: BSL 6.8 mmol/L, urine drug screen pending, ECG sinus tachycardia, QTc 430 ms

lead_in: What is the single best initial pharmacological management of his acute behavioural disturbance?

options.text: IM droperidol 10 mg with cautious midazolam 2.5 to 5 mg titrated, in a monitored area
options.rationale: Australian ED guidance for severe stimulant-related acute behavioural disturbance recommends parenteral droperidol with cautious adjunctive midazolam in a monitored setting, reflecting NSW Health protocols; this rapidly controls agitation while minimising oversedation (Source: NSW Health Management of Patients with Acute Severe Behavioural Disturbance; eTG Toxicology).
source_refs: NSW Health: Management of Patients with Acute Severe Behavioural Disturbance in ED

options.text: Oral diazepam 20 mg single dose, awaiting effect over the next 60 minutes
options.rationale: Oral medication is unsafe and impractical for severe ABD with imminent harm; parenteral in monitored area required. Would be correct only for mild agitation (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).
source_refs: NSW Health: Management of Patients with Acute Severe Behavioural Disturbance in ED

options.text: Intramuscular ketamine as first-line for acute behavioural sedation
options.rationale: Ketamine is reserved for refractory cases when droperidol and benzodiazepine have failed (laryngospasm, hypertension, airway risks). Would be correct only as refractory (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).
source_refs: NSW Health: Management of Patients with Acute Severe Behavioural Disturbance in ED

options.text: Intravenous haloperidol 5 mg as a single dose without concurrent benzodiazepine
options.rationale: IV haloperidol carries higher QT/torsades risk than IM droperidol and is not preferred in AU ABD protocols. Would be correct only if droperidol unavailable (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).
source_refs: NSW Health: Management of Patients with Acute Severe Behavioural Disturbance in ED

options.text: Physical restraint without any pharmacotherapy, awaiting natural resolution over hours
options.rationale: Prolonged physical restraint without sedation risks asphyxia, hyperthermia, rhabdomyolysis and sudden death in stimulant intoxication. Would never be correct (Source: ANZICS).
source_refs: NSW Health: Management of Patients with Acute Severe Behavioural Disturbance in ED

explanation.summary: Severe acute behavioural disturbance from methamphetamine is best managed with parenteral droperidol and cautious midazolam in a monitored area, with planning for stimulant-associated hyperthermia, rhabdomyolysis and cardiovascular complications (Source: NSW Health ABD Guideline; eTG Toxicology).
key_points: Treat the patient, monitor airway/breathing/circulation continuously after sedation.
Cool, hydrate, assess for rhabdomyolysis (CK), check ECG and electrolytes.
Avoid pure beta-blockade for stimulant hypertension (unopposed alpha effect); use benzodiazepines first.
Methamphetamine-induced psychosis typically resolves within 1 week with abstinence; persistent symptoms warrant psychiatric review.

sources.label: NSW Health: Management of Patients with Acute Severe Behavioural Disturbance in ED

stem: A 33-year-old woman is brought to ED by her partner. She has bipolar disorder and stopped lithium 3 weeks ago. Over the past 5 days she has slept 2 to 3 hours per night, has been spending savings on unnecessary purchases, and is now describing herself as 'chosen to save the city'. She has not slept in 36 hours and is pacing the ED. There is no current suicidal ideation or thoughts of harming others. She has no medical comorbidity, and a UDS and bloods are unremarkable. On MSE she is dressed flamboyantly, pressured in speech, with flight of ideas and grandiose delusions. She insists she is not unwell and refuses any treatment, including reinstating lithium. Her partner is present and supportive, and the community mental-health team has a current case manager

data_table.Examination: Elevated mood, pressured speech, flight of ideas, grandiose delusions, no current SI/HI, no psychomotor retardation
data_table.Investigations: FBC, UEC, LFTs, TSH normal, urine drug screen negative, beta-hCG negative, lithium level <0.2 mmol/L
data_table.Medications: Lithium ceased 3 weeks ago by patient; no current medications

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step under the NSW Mental Health Act 2007 (or equivalent state Act)?

options.text: Detain under the MHA assessment schedule and admit to a declared mental health facility for review
options.rationale: She meets criteria as a mentally ill person (presence of mental illness, behaviour requires treatment to protect her from serious harm including risk to her health/safety such as exhaustion, financial harm) and is refusing voluntary admission; the MHA assessment schedule and admission to a declared facility for AMO review is the appropriate pathway (Source: NSW Mental Health Act 2007; RANZCP).

options.text: Discharge home with her partner and arrange outpatient review with the case manager tomorrow
options.rationale: Severe sleep deprivation, escalating manic behaviour and refusal of treatment make outpatient unsafe in next 24 h. Would never be correct (Source: NSW MHA).

options.text: Apply common-law restraint and sedate her in the ED without using the Mental Health Act
options.rationale: Common-law restraint is for emergency intervention to prevent immediate harm, not primary framework for ongoing detention and treatment. Would be correct only briefly during emergency (Source: NSW MHA).

options.text: Make a community treatment order today without proceeding to any inpatient admission
options.rationale: CTOs are made by MHRT following inpatient assessment with specific procedural steps; cannot be made directly from ED. Would never be correct (Source: NSW MHA).

options.text: Document her refusal of care and allow discharge from ED against medical advice
options.rationale: She lacks capacity to refuse treatment due to grandiose delusions and lack of insight; MHA exists to address this. Would never be correct (Source: NSW MHA).

explanation.summary: Acute mania with refusal of treatment and risk to self meets criteria for involuntary admission under state MHAs. The least-restrictive principle still applies, but inpatient assessment is required before any community treatment order can be considered (Source: NSW Mental Health Act 2007).
key_points: Schedules/forms vary by state but principles are similar: presence of mental illness, risk of serious harm, no less restrictive option.
Common-law restraint is for immediate, brief safety - not ongoing treatment.
Community treatment orders follow tribunal processes after inpatient assessment.
Capacity is decision-specific and is impaired by acute mania with grandiose delusions.

stem: A 58-year-old woman with schizophrenia has been treated with first-generation antipsychotics for 25 years (most recently haloperidol 10 mg daily) and is being reviewed in the outpatient clinic. She has new-onset chewing movements, lip-smacking and occasional protrusion of the tongue for the past 4 months, occurring while not consciously moving and persisting during conversation. She has no oculogyric movements, no postural instability, no rigidity. AIMS examination scores her in the moderate severity range with involuntary orofacial movements. She is currently otherwise stable in her mental state. There is no history of cognitive impairment, and she has no recent change in medication. She has tried benztropine in the past for these movements without benefit She remains on her depot haloperidol despite the tardive dyskinesia meeting AIMS criteria. The treating psychiatrist is reviewing whether to switch agents Her family is involved and supportive.

data_table.Examination: Orofacial dyskinesia, normal limb tone and reflexes, no tremor, no rigidity
data_table.Investigations: AIMS score elevated in oromandibular domain; FBC, UEC, LFTs normal
data_table.Medications: Haloperidol 10 mg daily, trial of benztropine without benefit

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Increase oral benztropine to 4 mg daily for the tardive movements
options.rationale: Anticholinergics do not improve and may worsen TD; benztropine helps acute dystonia and drug-induced parkinsonism, not TD. Would be correct in acute dystonia or parkinsonism (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: Switch from haloperidol to a second-generation antipsychotic, ideally clozapine
options.rationale: Long-term first-generation antipsychotic exposure is the major risk factor for TD; switching to a second-generation antipsychotic with lower TD risk (clozapine has the strongest evidence) is the recommended strategy (Source: eTG Psychotropic; RANZCP Schizophrenia CPG 2016).

options.text: Add oral risperidone alongside the current haloperidol regimen
options.rationale: Polypharmacy increases adverse-effect burden including TD; combining second D2 antagonist worsens pathology. Would never be correct (Source: RANZCP).

options.text: Increase haloperidol dose further to suppress the involuntary movements
options.rationale: Increasing offending antipsychotic transiently masks TD but worsens long-term course; contraindicated as management. Would never be correct (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: Stop all antipsychotic therapy completely and observe over weeks
options.rationale: Abrupt cessation can cause withdrawal dyskinesia and risks psychotic relapse; switching to lower-risk agent preferred. Would never be correct as planned strategy (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).

explanation.summary: TD presents months to years after antipsychotic use as involuntary orofacial movements. First-line management is to switch to a lower-TD-risk antipsychotic (clozapine has the strongest evidence). VMAT2 inhibitors (valbenazine, deutetrabenazine) are emerging but not yet PBS-listed in Australia for routine TD treatment (Source: eTG Psychotropic).
key_points: AIMS assessed 6-monthly in patients on long-term antipsychotics.
Anticholinergics can worsen TD; cease them if possible.
Increasing the offending agent only masks the disorder.
Risk factors: older age, female, first-generation antipsychotics, long duration, prior EPSE.

stem: A 41-year-old man on clozapine 500 mg nocte for treatment-resistant schizophrenia missed 4 consecutive days of doses while travelling for a family funeral. He is asymptomatic, no longer psychotic, and feels well, but is keen to resume his usual dose tonight. He has a history of one previous generalised tonic-clonic seizure 5 years ago at clozapine 600 mg daily, which prompted dose reduction. He has no other neurological history. His mother (a nurse) phones the clozapine service requesting advice. Bloods this morning show normal FBC (WCC 6.8, neutrophils 4.0). He takes no other regular medications, weighs 92 kg, has BMI 30, and smokes 20 cigarettes daily. He cannot easily reach the clozapine prescriber until 48 hours from now and his community pharmacy has his usual 500 mg tablet ready The clozapine service consultant on call confirms that any break of more than 48 hours in clozapine requires re-titration from 12.5 mg per the Australian Clozapine Protocols, because the rapid loss of receptor adaptation and the well-known dose-related seizure and cardiovascular risks make restarting at his previous maintenance dose hazardous. She emphasises that this protocol applies even when the patient feels clinically well and asymptomatic, and that retitration is the same process used at first initiation, with monitoring of vital signs and observation for orthostatic hypotension, sedation, tachycardia and seizure activity during each step.

data_table.Examination: BMI 30, no neurological signs, no fever
data_table.Investigations: WCC 6.8, neutrophils 4.0, UEC normal, glucose 5.8 mmol/L; ECG sinus rhythm QTc 420 ms
data_table.Medications: Clozapine 500 mg nocte (4 doses missed), no other regular meds

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Restart at the usual 500 mg dose tonight without any retitration
options.rationale: After 48+ h without clozapine, tolerance is lost; resuming previous full dose markedly increases orthostatic hypotension, seizure and sedation risk. Would never be correct (Source: AMH).

options.text: Resume retitration from 12.5 mg per the standard induction protocol
options.rationale: Australian and manufacturer guidance is that after 2 or more days of missed clozapine doses, the drug must be re-titrated from the starting dose because tolerance to orthostatic hypotension, sedation and seizure risk is rapidly lost; this is particularly important given his prior seizure history (Source: Clozapine product information TGA; eTG Psychotropic; RANZCP).

options.text: Halve the previous dose to 250 mg nocte tonight and resume usual schedule
options.rationale: Halving does not safely re-establish tolerance and still carries significant risk; protocol requires retitration from 12.5 mg. Would never be correct (Source: AMH).

options.text: Switch immediately from clozapine to olanzapine at the equivalent oral dose
options.rationale: Switching from clozapine should be planned (relapse risk high); unplanned switch is unsafe and not indicated for missed-dose period. Would never be correct (Source: RANZCP).

options.text: Add prophylactic sodium valproate before restarting clozapine at any dose
options.rationale: Anticonvulsant prophylaxis is considered for higher-risk patients during retitration but does not replace requirement to retitrate from start. Would be correct as adjunct, not replacement (Source: RANZCP).

explanation.summary: Clozapine tolerance is rapidly lost; missed doses of 48 hours or more mandate full re-titration from the starting dose to avoid hypotension, sedation, seizures and cardiac events. Seizure risk is dose- and titration-related (Source: Clozapine TGA PI; eTG Psychotropic).
key_points: Restart from 12.5 mg if 2+ days missed; up-titrate cautiously per protocol.
Add prophylactic anticonvulsant in high-risk patients with prior clozapine-related seizures.
Smoking induces CYP1A2; smoking cessation raises clozapine levels.
Communicate any change in smoking, dose or interacting drugs to the clozapine service.

stem: A 64-year-old man admitted 3 days ago for orthopaedic surgery has not received scheduled benzodiazepine prophylaxis despite his documented daily alcohol intake of approximately 20 standard drinks. Last drink was 72 hours ago. He is now confused, agitated, picking at his bedding, and describing animals running across the ceiling. He has had two witnessed brief generalised tonic-clonic seizures in the past 4 hours. He is febrile, tachycardic, hypertensive, and diaphoretic. CIWA-Ar is now uninterpretable due to confusion. He has a wound dressing intact, no neurological focal deficits, and CT brain is unremarkable. He has received total diazepam 30 mg over the past 6 hours with limited effect. He has known cirrhosis (Child-Pugh B), albumin 28, INR 1.6, AST 130, ALT 70, platelets 95

data_table.Vital signs: T 38.4, HR 140, BP 174/100, RR 28, SpO2 95% on room air
data_table.Examination: Confused, GCS 13 (E3 V4 M6), tremulous, diaphoretic, visual hallucinations, no focal neurology, post-op wound dressed
data_table.Investigations: Na 132, K 3.0, Mg 0.58, glucose 5.6, lactate 3.4, INR 1.6, albumin 28, AST 130, ALT 70, platelets 95, CT brain unremarkable
data_table.Medications: Diazepam total 30 mg PO over 6 hours, IV Pabrinex 2 pairs commenced

lead_in: What is the single best next step in management?

options.text: Transfer to ICU or HDU, escalate to IV diazepam (or lorazepam if hepatic impairment), correct electrolytes
options.rationale: Delirium tremens with refractory agitation and seizures is a medical emergency requiring high-acuity care; in significant hepatic impairment, lorazepam (less hepatic metabolism, no active metabolites) is preferred over diazepam, with aggressive electrolyte correction (Source: NSW Health Withdrawal Guidelines; eTG Addiction; eTG Critical Care).
source_refs: NSW Health Drug and Alcohol Withdrawal Clinical Practice Guidelines

options.text: Continue oral diazepam, increase the frequency and reassess his condition in 4 hours
options.rationale: Oral diazepam in confused, seizing, vomiting patient is unreliable; severe DT requires parenteral therapy and high-acuity monitoring. Would never be correct (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).
source_refs: NSW Health Drug and Alcohol Withdrawal Clinical Practice Guidelines

options.text: Commence intravenous haloperidol 10 mg as the primary treatment for the agitated withdrawal state
options.rationale: Antipsychotics lower seizure threshold and don't treat underlying GABA deficit; benzodiazepines are first-line in DT with low-dose antipsychotic only as adjunct. Would be correct only as adjunct (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).
source_refs: NSW Health Drug and Alcohol Withdrawal Clinical Practice Guidelines

options.text: Load with intravenous phenytoin 1 g as primary anti-seizure prophylaxis tonight
options.rationale: Phenytoin is ineffective for alcohol-withdrawal seizures (GABA-mediated); benzodiazepines are both treatment and prophylaxis. Would never be correct (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: Administer intramuscular naloxone empirically in case of unrecognised opioid involvement
options.rationale: Naloxone has no role in alcohol withdrawal; it is opioid antagonist for opioid overdose. Would never be correct in alcohol withdrawal (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).

explanation.summary: Delirium tremens has a mortality of 5 to 15% untreated. Australian management is escalation to high-acuity care, parenteral benzodiazepines (lorazepam preferred in significant hepatic impairment because of pharmacokinetics), and aggressive electrolyte correction with high-dose B-complex (Source: NSW Health Withdrawal Guidelines).
key_points: DT onset 48 to 96 hours after last drink; mortality without treatment up to 15%.
Lorazepam is preferred over diazepam in significant hepatic dysfunction.
Always replace thiamine before/with glucose and correct magnesium.
Antipsychotics only as adjunct for severe hallucinations after benzodiazepine optimisation.

sources.label: NSW Health Drug and Alcohol Withdrawal Clinical Practice Guidelines
